name: sec-review
description: Cybersecurity review plugin for web services, servers, browser extensions, Rust projects, Android apps, iOS apps, and desktop Linux targets: a pipelined eleven-agent architecture (sec-expert, finding-triager, cve-enricher, sast-runner, dast-runner, webext-runner, rust-runner, android-runner, ios-runner, linux-runner, report-writer) with model pinning — sonnet-grade code reasoning, haiku-grade CVE feed I/O and SAST/DAST/webext/rust/android/ios/linux tool dispatch. Citation-grounded expertise across databases, frameworks, webservers (including Windows/IIS as of v0.4.0), proxies, frontend, browser extensions (Chrome MV3 + Firefox AMO as of v0.6.0), the Rust/Cargo ecosystem (as of v0.7.0), Android (as of v0.8.0), iOS (as of v0.9.0), desktop Linux (systemd + sandboxing + .deb/.rpm packaging, as of v0.10.0), auth, TLS, containers, secrets, and supply-chain; live CVE enrichment from NVD 2.0, OSV.dev (native for crates.io and Maven; best-effort via GHSA for CocoaPods, SwiftPM, and Debian), and GHSA with CISA KEV cross-reference; SAST adapter invoking semgrep and bandit; DAST invoking OWASP ZAP baseline; webext lane invoking addons-linter, web-ext lint, and retire.js; Rust lane invoking cargo-audit, cargo-deny, cargo-geiger (INFO-ceiling), and cargo-vet; Android lane invoking mobsfscan, apkleaks, and android-lint; iOS lane invoking mobsfscan (cross-platform with Android) plus Apple's codesign/spctl/xcrun-notarytool on macOS hosts; Linux lane invoking systemd-analyze security (on systemd hosts), lintian (on debian/ source), and checksec (on ELF binaries) — with origin-tagged findings, three-state sentinels, and a structured skipped-list primitive distinguishing clean skips (target-shape: no-apk/no-bundle/no-debian-source/no-elf/no-systemd-unit; host-OS-gated: requires-macos-host/requires-systemd-host; profile-absent: no-notary-profile) from tool failures; per-run token-cost accounting; offline-degradation drills for CVE feeds and all seven tool lanes; prioritized markdown report with reliable, primary-source-cited fixes.
